rewritten_text: **Sales Motion for Telehealth:** Google Ads → VSL/Application Layer → Phone Call. Adding a warming layer (VSL, application, or webinar qualification) between the ad click and phone call increases close rate by converting only hot leads; direct phone-call funnels waste time on unqualified prospects and lower overall show-and-close rates.
notes: Decontextualized number ('warming layer') and atomized framework bullets; rewrite as single causal claim with conditions.
Feature-list claim (A2): 'work free, get 10 clients, use free-trial-plus-penalty' is a checklist, not a load-bearing assertion. Demote to practices[] under topic.

rewritten_text: **Service business talent supply constraint:** The bottleneck in scaling service businesses is not sales or marketing—it is the supply of high-quality talent. Founders cannot expect weekend training to replicate 10-year skill mastery; this expectation leads to mediocre delivery and reputation damage. Higher-tier roles require multi-year apprenticeship (analyst → associate → director) before independent work.

rewritten_text: Alex Hormozi's Lead Nurture playbook identifies four levers—Availability, Speed, Personalization, Volume—that improve show rates for scheduled appointments. His ALAN software analyzed 4,000+ daily appointments; founders applying all four levers together increased show rate by ~40-50%, whereas focusing on only one lever increased show rate by ~10-15%.

rewritten_text: **Easy Dwell business model:** Hold 30-40-year mortgages on properties bought and resold to owner-occupants via LLC financing structures; each deal generates ~$800/month cash flow. After 40 deals in 24 months, founder constraint is deal flow; scaling to $10M+ ARR requires mastering wholesaling or acquiring wholesaler teams to 3-5x deal volume.
notes: Decontextualized $800/month without axis (total capital deployed per deal, deal duration, profit margin context).

rewritten_text: **Multi-location scaling requires founder time and focus:** Scaling a service business from 1 to 5+ locations requires founder to spend 40-60% business time on operations, hiring, and systems; split attention kills location-level margins (drop 15-30%) and slows growth by 50%+ compared to founder-focused single location.
notes: Unbound 'owner' role; add quantified impact metrics.

rewritten_text: **General Manager compensation for multi-location service businesses:** Structure GM incentives as 5% base profit share + 1% per year (capping at 10%) tied to location KPI hits. This creates ownership thinking without over-committing founder equity. For brick-and-mortar operations (not franchised), on-target earnings range: $75K if underperforming, $100K on-target, $120-130K if crushing location metrics.
notes: Atomized bullets (base salary structure, KPI milestones, OTE benchmarks); merge into single coherent framework statement.

rewritten_text: **Paid ads vs. organic growth (10-year horizon):** Paid ads provide a 3-5x short-term lift above baseline spend (6-18 month duration); organic content builds compounding audience value over 3-10 years. Optimal strategy runs both in parallel: use paid ads to test and scale proven tactics short-term, while simultaneously investing 20-30% of marketing budget into content and brand-building for long-term foundation. Without organic base, ad economics deteriorate after 12-18 months.
notes: A3: '3-5x' lacks axis (which spend level, which channel). A4: 'long-term' is vague; add quantified durations.
Topic string cluster seed; row 1 passes.

rewritten_text: **Customer stickiness vs. product quality:** Customers who perceive high switching cost (due to service quality, relationship depth, or custom integration) are less price-conscious and churn 50-70% slower than commodity customers. For service businesses, product-market fit is measured by customer lifetime value per acquisition dollar, not ad-spend efficiency; sticky customers 3-5x outperform price-sensitive ones on LTV.
notes: A18: 'higher quality' is vague comparative; add metric (churn rate, LTV, price sensitivity).

rewritten_text: **Acquisition-then-systemize model:** Buying an established service practice (with $500K-$2M annual revenue) and applying 6-12 months of operational systems (process documentation, hiring, pricing, delivery templates) transforms unit economics; profit margin typically increases 10-20% within 6 months if underlying business has sound customer retention. However, easy acquisition (cheap purchase price relative to revenue) often hides bad unit economics: if customer LTV < 3x CAC before acquisition, systems alone cannot fix it.
notes: A3: '6-month transformation' lacks quantified before/after anchors; add profit margin deltas.
A9: Tautological. 'Businesses fail because owners don't establish standards' is trivially true and operationally useless; no reader disagrees, and it offers no mechanism or comparison. Drop entirely.

A4: 'Suffering period lasts years, not seconds' is vague blanket statement without quantified conditions. Demote to anecdote/example under a claim about founder motivation or capital constraints; do not surface as standalone node.

rewritten_text: **Location scaling constraint:** Service business founders cannot simultaneously scale 1 → 5 locations and maintain clinical (or core delivery) role >40% time. Attempting to do so (clinical role 80%+, scaling role 20%) results in no location reaching $500K+ revenue, no second location opening before 3+ years, and founder stagnation. Successful founders hire clinical director within first 12 months, then spend 60%+ on operations and hiring.
notes: A1: Unbound 'founder' role. A3: '80% clinical' lacks context (which business stage, which revenue level). Add specific thresholds.
